summary: Gruber examines the differences between Android Wear's new low-power ambient mode and Apple Watch's approach to display states. Android Wear keeps minimal information visible on screen at all times using black-and-white graphics, while Apple Watch turns the screen completely off when not in use. He argues that Apple's black-background aesthetic in the 'on' state was primarily a design choice—making the screen blend seamlessly into the bezel—rather than purely a battery optimization. He acknowledges that Android Wear's always-on ambient display is genuinely useful, referencing his own prior complaint about needing to move his wrist to check the time on Apple Watch.
key_points: Android Wear's ambient mode keeps minimal information visible at all times, while Apple Watch displays nothing when 'off'—a clear battery life concession
Apple Watch uses black backgrounds throughout its UI to create the illusion that the screen and bezel are one continuous surface, a deliberate design choice enabled by OLED technology
Google's Android Wear aesthetic mirrors its broader Material Design language with colorful, white-background UIs, making it look like 'a version of Android running on a watch'
The always-on ambient display addresses a real usability gap that Gruber himself identified with Apple Watch—the inability to glance at the time without a wrist gesture
OLED's property of black pixels consuming no power makes both approaches viable, but Apple and Google prioritize differently between aesthetics and utility
money_quotes: A fundamental aspect of Apple Watch's design is that you can almost never see where the screen ends and the surrounding bezel begins.
It's a compelling, elegant effect. The battery-life advantages of this design are just a nice side effect.
Apple's approach is more conservative energy-wise in both 'on' and 'off' states. Google's leaves something informative on screen at all times.
It looks like what its name implies: a version of Android running on a watch.
Apple's decision to have the screen display nothing while 'off' was clearly a concession to battery life.
key_insight: Apple Watch's all-black aesthetic is primarily a design decision to make screen and bezel indistinguishable, with battery savings as a fortunate byproduct, while Android Wear trades that elegance for the practical utility of always-visible information.
